old_claim.note: Water-Powered Trip Hammers & Forges provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: A dam and wooden water wheel supplied the mechanical power for the Lapphyttan reconstruction's leather bellows, making watermill machinery the direct power prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: ERIH describes the reconstructed Lapphyttan furnace as having a dam to store water, a wooden water wheel, and leather bellows to keep the fire hot.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source documents water storage, a water wheel, and leather bellows as components of the water-powered blast-furnace reconstruction.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing the trip-hammer edge because water-wheel machinery, not hammer-forge machinery, is the component that directly powers the blast-furnace bellows.

ontology_before: Water-Powered Blast Furnaces depended on Water-Powered Trip Hammers & Forges, implying hammer/forge machinery was the prerequisite water-power lineage.
ontology_after: Water-Powered Blast Furnaces depends directly on watermills for water-wheel power, while trip hammers remain a separate water-powered forge application.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: water_powered_blast_furnaces now depends on watermills and no longer directly depends on water_powered_trip_hammers_forges.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to integrated forge complexes where trip hammers were necessary to operate the furnace installation.
A stronger source showed medieval water-powered blast furnaces required trip hammers rather than only water-wheel-powered bellows.
The watermills node were narrowed away from water-wheel power machinery.
short_reason: Water-wheel power runs the bellows; trip hammers are parallel forge machinery.
